title: Articles — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: a / an / the / zero article — the most-missed grammar point in IELTS
intro: Articles are the most frequently wrong feature in non-native English. Even Band 7+ candidates lose marks here. Bangla, Hindi, Urdu, Korean, Mandarin and Russian speakers face the most challenge — none of these languages have a/an/the system. The good news: 90% of article errors fall under 5 patterns. Master these and you eliminate the single largest grammar leak.

sections.title: 1. The Three-Article System
sections.summary: English has three article choices: indefinite (a/an), definite (the), and zero (no article). Every countable singular noun MUST take one — there is no fourth option.

rules.pattern: A — used before consonant SOUND (not letter)

examples.plural: a book, a car, a university (yoo-niversity), a European country (yoo-ropean), a one-way street (won-way)

rules.note: It's about the SOUND, not the spelling. 'A university' starts with /j/ sound. 'An hour' starts with vowel /aʊ/.

examples.plural: an apple, an idea, an hour (silent h), an MBA (em-bee-ay), an FBI agent (ef-bee-ai), an honest person

rules.pattern: THE — for specific / shared knowledge / unique

examples.plural: I bought a book. The book was expensive.
the sun, the moon, the world, the internet, the environment
the book on the table, the man in the blue shirt
the best, the most important, the first, the only
Children need love. (NOT 'The children need the love.')
Education is essential. (NOT 'The education is essential.')
Water is vital for life. (NOT 'The water is vital.')

sections.title: 2. When to Use A / AN
sections.summary: Indefinite article = first mention, one of many, classifying.

rules.pattern: First mention of a singular countable noun

examples.plural: I saw a dog in the park.
Pick a card.

rules.pattern: Naming a profession, role, or category

examples.plural: She is a teacher / a doctor / an engineer.
A whale is a mammal. (= one of the mammals)
He is an American. / She is a Brazilian.

rules.note: Common Bangla/Indian-English error: 'She is teacher' (no article). English requires 'a' before profession nouns.

examples.plural: twice a week, three times a day, $10 an hour, 60 km an hour
a few, a little, a lot, a couple of, a number of
What a day! / What a beautiful view!

sections.title: 3. When to Use THE
sections.summary: Definite article = the listener/reader knows WHICH ONE. Specific, unique, or already-mentioned.

examples.plural: A man walked in. The man was tall.
the sun, the moon, the earth, the world, the universe
the internet, the environment, the economy, the climate, the government
the north, the south, the east, the west, the left, the right
the capital of France, the population of Bangladesh
the book that I bought, the woman who lives next door
the man on the train, the pen in my pocket
the best, the worst, the most important, the largest
the first, the second, the last, the third time
the only solution, the only person who can help
The elephant is endangered. (= elephants as a species)
She plays the piano / the violin / the guitar.
The telephone changed communication. The internet has transformed society.

rules.note: Generic 'the' + singular has a formal/scientific feel. The plural without 'the' (Elephants are endangered) sounds more natural in Task 2.

examples.plural: the Ganges, the Nile, the Mediterranean, the Pacific Ocean
the Sahara, the Himalayas, the Alps, the Bahamas
the United States, the UK, the Netherlands, the Philippines
the rich, the poor, the elderly, the homeless, the unemployed (all take plural verb)

sections.title: 4. When to Use NO Article (Zero Article)
sections.summary: Zero article = general / abstract / uncountable. This is the single most-missed pattern by Bangla/Indian English speakers.

rules.pattern: Plural countable nouns in GENERAL sense

examples.plural: Children need love. (NOT 'The children…')
Cars cause pollution. (NOT 'The cars cause the pollution.')
Books are valuable. (NOT 'The books are valuable.')

rules.note: Adding 'the' makes it specific: 'The children in this school' = a specific group, not children generally.

examples.plural: Education is important. (NOT 'The education is important.')
Water is essential. (NOT 'The water is essential.' — unless specific)
Happiness comes from within. / Knowledge is power.
Bangladesh, India, France, China, Tokyo, Asia, Africa

rules.note: Plural-form country names take 'the': the United States, the UK, the Philippines.

rules.pattern: Names of meals, languages, sports, academic subjects

examples.plural: I have breakfast at 8. (NOT 'the breakfast')
She speaks French. (NOT 'the French')
He plays football / cricket / tennis.
I study mathematics / physics / economics.

rules.pattern: Set phrases — institutions used for their PURPOSE

examples.plural: go to school, go to bed, go to church, go to hospital (BrE), be in prison, be at home, go to work
go to the school (visit the building), go to the hospital (AmE), the church next door

rules.note: 'My son is at school' = he's a student / studying. 'My son is at THE school' = he's at the building (perhaps to fix something).

examples.plural: by car, by train, by plane, by bus, by bicycle, on foot

rules.note: But: 'in the car / on the train / on the bus' (when describing being inside a specific vehicle).

sections.title: 5. Articles with Abstract Nouns — The Hardest Pattern
sections.summary: Abstract nouns (education, life, society, nature) take ZERO article when general — but THE when specified. This is where Band 7 candidates most often slip.

examples.plural: Education is the key to development. (NOT 'The education')
Life is challenging. (NOT 'The life')
Nature provides everything we need.
Society values material success.
Crime is increasing. / Poverty affects millions.
the education of girls in rural areas (specific)
the life of a refugee (specific)
the nature of the problem (specific)
the society of ancient Egypt (specific)

rules.pattern: Abstract → specific shift in the same essay

examples.plural: Education is fundamental. → The education provided in rural schools is often inadequate.
Pollution is a global concern. → The pollution caused by factories has worsened local air quality.

rules.note: Same word, two articles, depending on whether the meaning is general or specific. This is the Band 7+ control examiners look for.

sections.title: 6. Tricky Article Cases (Band 7+ Specific)
sections.summary: Cases that even strong candidates miss.

rules.pattern: MAN / MANKIND / WOMAN as humanity → no article

examples.plural: Man has lived on Earth for thousands of years.
Mankind faces unprecedented challenges.

rules.note: Modern preference: 'humanity / human beings / humankind' instead of 'man'. But the article rule is the same — no 'the'.

rules.pattern: PLAY + the + instrument; PLAY + sport (no article)

examples.plural: She plays the piano / the violin / the flute.
She plays football / chess / cricket. (NOT 'the football')

examples.singular: ❌ a my book / the my book
examples.plural: ✔ my book
✔ his idea

rules.note: If you need both, use 'a / one of': 'a book of mine' / 'one of my books'.

examples.plural: the Nile, the Atlantic, the Bay of Bengal, the Suez Canal
Lake Victoria, Mount Everest, Sri Lanka
the Times, the Guardian, the New York Times
the Hilton, the Ritz
in the 1990s, in the early 2000s, since the 1950s
in the 19th century
I have a few friends here. (= I have some)
I have few friends here. (= almost no friends)
We have a little time. (= some)
We have little time. (= almost no time)

rules.note: Dropping the 'a' completely changes the meaning to negative. Common Band 6 confusion.

sections.title: 7. Articles in IELTS Writing — Topic-Specific Patterns
sections.summary: Common IELTS Task 2 abstract topics and how to handle their articles correctly.

examples.plural: Education plays a vital role in development.
The education system in Finland is widely admired.
Students benefit from extra-curricular activities.
The students at this school have access to advanced facilities.
The environment is under threat. / The climate is changing.
Pollution causes serious health problems.
Renewable energy is the future.
Society has changed dramatically over recent decades.
The society of the 21st century is digitally connected.
The government must take action. / The authorities have introduced new measures.
Governments around the world are facing similar challenges.
Health is more important than wealth.
The health of urban populations has declined.
Heart disease is the leading cause of death globally.

sections.tip: Audit your last Task 2 essay: every time you wrote 'the education / the pollution / the society / the people in general' — check whether 'the' actually points to something specific. If not, delete it.

sections.title: 8. Top Article Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: These are the specific article mistakes that pull Band 7 candidates down.

examples.plural: ✔ Education is important.

examples.singular: ❌ The life is full of challenges.
examples.plural: ✔ Life is full of challenges.
✔ Nature is beautiful.
✔ My father is an engineer.
✔ She wants to be a teacher.
✔ Children love sweets. (children in general)
✔ Cars cause pollution.

rules.pattern: 4. Wrong choice of a / an before vowel/consonant SOUND

examples.plural: ✔ an hour / an honest mistake (silent h)
✔ a university / a European country (yoo- sound)

examples.singular: ❌ an MBA seems wrong but isn't
examples.plural: ✔ an MBA (em-bee-ay = vowel sound 'em')
✔ The sun rises in the east.
✔ The government must act.
✔ The internet has changed our lives.

rules.pattern: 6. 'The' before names of single countries / cities

examples.singular: ❌ I live in the Bangladesh / the India / the China.
examples.plural: ✔ I live in Bangladesh / India / China.
✔ I visited London.

rules.note: Exception: plural-form countries DO take 'the' — the United States, the UK, the Netherlands.

examples.singular: ❌ I want a homework / a furniture / an information.
examples.plural: ✔ I want some homework / some furniture / some information.

examples.singular: ❌ I saw the dog in the park yesterday. (random unspecified dog)
examples.plural: ✔ I saw a dog in the park yesterday. (first mention of unknown dog)

rules.pattern: 9. 'Few / little' vs 'a few / a little' meaning shift

examples.singular: ❌ I have few friends here, so I'm happy. (logically wrong — few = almost none)
examples.plural: ✔ I have a few friends here, so I'm happy. (a few = some)

rules.pattern: 10. Article before 'play + sport' or 'play + instrument' confusion

examples.singular: ❌ I play the cricket / the football.
examples.plural: ✔ I play cricket / football.
✔ She plays the piano in concerts.

sections.tip: Print this 10-error list. Run a one-pass article audit on every essay you write for the next 14 days — specifically asking 'Is this noun GENERAL or SPECIFIC?' for every 'the' you used and every noun you left bare.

checklist: I use 'a' before consonant SOUNDS and 'an' before vowel SOUNDS, not letters (a university, an hour, an MBA).
I never put 'the' before abstract nouns in general statements (Education is important, NOT The education).
I never put 'the' before plural general nouns (Children play, NOT The children play in general).
I never put 'the' before single-country names (Bangladesh, India), but I do for plural-form ones (the UK, the US).
I use 'the' for unique entities (the sun, the government, the internet, the environment).
I use 'a/an' before profession nouns (She is a doctor, NOT She is doctor).
I use 'the' + adjective for social groups (the rich, the elderly).
I distinguish 'few' (almost none) from 'a few' (some); 'little' (almost none) from 'a little' (some).
I use 'play the piano' (instrument) but 'play football' (sport) without article.
I never use 'a' with uncountable nouns (a furniture, an information) — I use 'some' or a partitive instead.
I shift correctly between abstract general (Pollution is bad) and specific (The pollution caused by factories).
